title: Österreichischer Staatsschatzschein über Fünftausend Kronen (Austrian State Treasury Note for Five Thousand Crowns)
description: A bearer treasury note issued by the Austrian state for 5,000 Kronen, dated September 1, 1920 in Vienna, bearing serial number 008255 in Series 1920b. The note is designated as tax-free (steuerfrei) and interest-bearing, with provisions for redemption by the state debt administration. The document features elaborate green and pink decorative borders with floral motifs, a double-headed Austrian imperial eagle at the top center, and ornamental medallions at each corner. Issued in the immediate aftermath of World War I and the dissolution of the Habsburg Empire, this bond represents the new Austrian republic's efforts to raise capital during a period of severe economic instability and hyperinflation.
creator: Republic of Austria, Österreichische Staatsschuldenverwaltung
notes: Austrian State Treasury Note, Serie 1920b, No. 008,255, 5,000 Kronen, tax-free. Vienna, September 1, 1920. Signed by the Staatsschuldenverwaltung.

title: Burmese Government Bond
description: A Burmese government bond written entirely in Burmese script, featuring green and purple decorative borders with traditional floral and geometric motifs. The document displays the serial number 008358 in red numerals on both sides and a central circular vignette with the denomination printed in Burmese characters. The footer contains a date in the Burmese calendar system, suggesting issuance during the 1940s when Burma operated as a nominally independent state under Japanese occupation. The design incorporates traditional Burmese decorative elements including intricate petal and leaf motifs framing the central text, in a style consistent with official financial instruments of the era. This is a rare surviving example of financial documentation from wartime Burma, notable for its exclusive use of the indigenous script at a politically turbulent moment in Burmese history.
creator: Government of Burma
notes: Approximate date ca. 1943-1945 based on historical context of Japanese-occupied Burma

title: 5% Vutreshen Durzaven Zaem ot 1941g. za Narodnata Otbrana (5% Internal State Loan of 1941 for National Defense, Kingdom of Bulgaria)
description: A bearer bond issued by the Kingdom of Bulgaria ("Tsarstvo Bulgaria") for the 5% Internal State Loan of 1941, explicitly designated for national defense, valued at 5,000 leva, Series B, serial number 022882. The bond is issued under the authority of the Main Directorate of State and State-Guaranteed Debts and signed by senior officials. The document features a cream and brown color scheme with ornate circular decorations at each corner and a central panel prominently displaying the denomination of 5,000 leva alongside the 5% interest rate. Issued during World War II when Bulgaria was aligned with the Axis powers under Tsar Boris III, this bond was intended to fund military expenditure. The phrase "Na Prinositelya" (to the bearer) confirms it was a negotiable bearer instrument payable to whoever presented it.
creator: Kingdom of Bulgaria, Main Directorate of State Debts
notes: Approximate issue date; exact date not visible on document

title: Hoe de Assurantie-Compagnie van Rotterdam kan werden gevodert, en dat niemant zou verliezen (How the Insurance Company of Rotterdam Can Be Promoted, and That No One Would Lose)
description: Page 1 of 7 – The first page of a seven-page Dutch pamphlet proposing the establishment of a Rotterdam insurance company during the speculative bubble of 1720, presenting numbered articles (1 through 7) outlining how investors could participate without risk of loss. The text is set in a simple typographic style with an ornamental initial capital, characteristic of Dutch commercial printing of the early eighteenth century. This document was included in "Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid" (The Great Mirror of Folly), a famous Dutch compilation documenting the speculative mania of 1720. The title's promise that "no one would lose" is characteristic of the optimistic—and often misleading—claims made by company promoters during this financial bubble. The pamphlet reflects the proliferation of prospectuses during this period, when hundreds of speculative ventures were promoted across the Dutch Republic.

title: Conditien van de Maatschappy tot het Asseureeren van Scheepen en Goederen, Binnen de Stad Middelburg (Conditions of the Company for Insuring Ships and Goods within the City of Middelburg)
description: A single-page printed broadsheet presenting the conditions of a maritime insurance company established within the city of Middelburg in the province of Zeeland, the Netherlands, set in two columns with numbered articles covering membership, share subscriptions, claims procedures, and governance. Middelburg was a major Dutch trading port and the capital of Zeeland, making it a natural center for maritime insurance ventures during the speculative climate of 1720. The two-column layout and Roman typeface are typical of Dutch commercial printing of the early eighteenth century, and the numbered articles present the company's terms in a formal legalistic manner intended to convey legitimacy. This document is likely associated with the speculative companies promoted during the Dutch financial bubble of 1720 and is preserved in the "Groote Tafereel" collection. The broadsheet format and dense text suggest it was intended for public posting or distribution to prospective investors.
creator: Maatschappy tot het Asseureeren van Scheepen en Goederen, Middelburg
notes: Likely from Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid (1720); exact page count uncertain

title: Reglement op de Wisselbank Binnen Utrecht (Regulations for the Exchange Bank within Utrecht), 1720
description: Page 1 of 7 – The title page of a seven-page document establishing the Exchange Bank ("Wisselbank") within the city of Utrecht, adopted by the city council on 7 October 1720 and formally published on 11 October by Jacob van Poolsum, the official city printer ("Stads Drukker"). The page displays the Utrecht city coat of arms flanked by heraldic supporters, marking the document as an official civic publication. Exchange banks modeled on the Amsterdam Wisselbank were established in several Dutch cities during the early eighteenth century as stabilizing financial institutions for commerce. This document was issued at the height of the 1720 Dutch financial bubble, reflecting the city's effort to create a reliable monetary institution amid speculative chaos. The formal civic authorization underscores the role of Dutch municipal governments in regulating financial activity during the early modern period.

title: Inventaris van de Effecten, behorende aan de Colonie de Barbice (Inventory of Assets Belonging to the Colony of Berbice)
description: A Dutch inventory document listing the assets ("effecten") of the Colony of Berbice (present-day Guyana), including 895 enslaved people described as large and small ("groote en kleyne Slaven"), cacao plantations, Fort Nassau, military equipment including sixty cannon pieces, ships, weapons, livestock, medical supplies, and a church. The document is formatted as a numbered list of asset categories with quantities and estimated values, reflecting the bookkeeping conventions of Dutch colonial enterprise. The page number 574 visible at the bottom indicates this is an extract from a larger bound volume, likely related to speculative colonial investment promoted during the 1720 Dutch financial bubble. Berbice was a Dutch colony on the northern coast of South America administered under a chartered company structure, and this inventory reflects the entanglement of colonial enterprise, enslaved labor, and early modern financial speculation. The document is a stark record of the commodification of enslaved people alongside material assets in Dutch colonial accounting.
creator: Colony of Berbice / Dutch West India Company
notes: Extract from larger bound volume (page 574); likely from Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid (1720) or related colonial prospectus

title: Project tot het ruineeren der Turkze Rovers van Miers, Tunis, Tripoly en Sale (Project for the Ruin of the Turkish Pirates of Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli and Sale)
description: Page 1 of 8 – The title page of a Dutch pamphlet proposing a project to eliminate ("ruineeren") the Barbary pirates ("Turkze Rovers") of Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli, and Sale, framed as beneficial not only for the security of Dutch shipping but also for the expansion of navigation and commerce across these trade routes. The title emphasizes dual objectives: protecting Dutch merchant vessels from North African piracy and freeing Dutch captive subjects held in the Barbary states. The document is addressed to the States General and other authorities of the Dutch Republic, presenting the project as a matter of national economic and humanitarian interest. Barbary piracy had long posed a serious threat to European Mediterranean and Atlantic trade, and numerous proposals for its suppression circulated in the early eighteenth century. This pamphlet represents the intersection of Dutch commercial ambition, maritime security concerns, and the speculative project culture of the 1720 period.

title: Temple Mills (South Sea Bubble Playing Card, King of Diamonds)
description: A playing card from the English South Sea Bubble satirical deck depicting Temple Mills, showing workers at a water mill with figures carrying sacks while a king of diamonds is inset in the upper left corner. The satirical verse reads: "By these Old Mills, Strange Wonders have been done / Numbers have Suffer'd, yet they Still Work on; / Then tell us which have done the Greater Ills, / The Temple Lawyers, or the Temple Mills." The card draws a sardonic comparison between the exploitative practices of lawyers practicing at the Temple in London and a fraudulent speculative venture promoting the Temple Mills site as an investment opportunity. The South Sea Bubble playing card deck was published around 1720 to mock the proliferation of speculative schemes and the gullibility of investors during the financial frenzy that preceded the collapse of the South Sea Company. Each card in the deck depicts a different fraudulent or impractical scheme, using satirical verses and engraved illustrations to ridicule both the promoters and their victims.

title: Dutch Windkaart: King of Spades
description: This engraved playing card depicts the King of Spades (Heer) from a Dutch satirical card deck mocking the speculative bubble mania of 1720. A gentleman in fashionable dress stands holding a balance scale, weighing a shovel and sheet of paper against a purse of gold coins. The Dutch verse reads: "De schop, en't blad papier in de eene schaal gelegen, / Kan, door de braversshoop, een goud beurs overwegen" (The shovel and a sheet of paper in one pan of the scale / Can, through a gambler's luck, outweigh a purse of gold). The card satirizes speculative investment in stock company shares ("Acties"), suggesting that worthless paper backed only by luck could appear to rival real wealth.

title: English Lottery Trade Card: Fortune's Carriage
description: This printed lottery trade card features a wood-engraved vignette of a horse-drawn carriage bearing the royal cypher "GR" (Georgius Rex), driven by Fortune, who raises a lottery ticket aloft. The text promotes a State Lottery offering prizes including three of £30,000 and four of £20,000, totaling £314,460 "In Stock and Money," sold by Swift at 1 Poultry, 12 Charing Cross, and 31 Aldgate High Street, London. The drawing date noted is 7th November. These small promotional cards were distributed widely to advertise British State Lottery drawings, which operated as a major source of government revenue from the late 17th century until their abolition in 1826.

title: French Royal Tontine Certificate, 10th Tontine, 1759 (Page 1 of 2)
description: This handwritten and printed tontine certificate is issued under the "10ème Tontine créée par Édit du mois de Décembre 1759" (10th Royal Tontine created by Edict of December 1759). The subscriber is "Jean Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Vauzette, Bourgeois de Paris," who has paid "Deux cent Livres" (200 livres) for a share, with the annuity contingent on the life of "Victoire Sophie de Noailles," described as sixty years old and widow of a member of the household of Alexandre de Bourbon, Comte de Toulouse. Annuities are to be paid twice yearly at the Hôtel des Marchands et Échevins (Merchants' and Aldermen's Hall) of Paris, as established by the Royal Edict and the regulations of the Ferme générale. Signed by Joseph Micault d'Harvelay, Conseiller du Roi and Garde du Trésor royal. Royal tontines were a major mechanism of French state borrowing throughout the 18th century, raising capital by pooling life annuities that increased as fellow subscribers died.

pages.description: Page 1 of 2 - Tontine, 1760. Part of the Pierre Brissot de Warville collection
Page 2 pf 2 - Tontine, 1760

title: United States Continental Lottery Subscriber List, Winchester, Virginia, 1777 (Page 1 of 3)
description: This handwritten manuscript is the first page of a United States Continental Lottery ticket subscriber list compiled in Winchester, Virginia, recording names, ticket quantities, and assigned lot numbers (prefixed "52m"). Subscribers include John Hanly (2 tickets), John Norvell (4), William C. Holliday (3), Albert Roberts (1), John Cox (3), Edward M. Quinn (3), John DuHeld (2), Benjamin Williams, Ann Brownson, Elizabeth McFarland, Col. Isaac Lake (10 tickets), and others—totaling at least 86 tickets carried over to the next page. The tickets were sold on behalf of Josiah Watson Esq. of Alexandria, Virginia, who distributed United States Lottery tickets to local agents in the Shenandoah Valley for sale to the public. This list is part of a small archive documenting how Continental Lottery ticket sales were organized through a decentralized network of agents during the Revolutionary War.

List records names of purchasers, numbers of tickets, and sale price. Appended to list is a note from Holliday to James Searle.

title: United States Continental Lottery Ticket Sales List, Robert Traill, Easton, 1778 (Page 1 of 2)
description: This handwritten manuscript is headed "United States Lottery, Class the Second, List of Tickets sold by Robert Traill of Easton." It records 25 lottery ticket numbers (prefixed "gom") and concludes: "25 Tickets, 20 Dollars each, 500 Dollars." Signed "Rob. Traill, Easton, Decmr. 27, 1778." The United States Continental Lottery was authorized by resolution of Congress on November 18, 1776 to help finance the Revolutionary War, and was organized in successive classes (First, Second, Third) with tickets priced at $20 each. Robert Traill operated as a local lottery agent in Easton, Pennsylvania, distributing and selling tickets on behalf of the lottery commissioners in Philadelphia. This list is evidence of how the Continental Lottery reached small-town markets throughout Pennsylvania through a network of named local agents.

title: United States Continental Lottery Ticket, Class the Third, No. 1A.m.655, 1776 (Page 1 of 2)
description: This printed lottery ticket reads: "United States Lottery No. 1A.m.655 – CLASS the THIRD. THIS TICKET entitles the Bearer to receive such PRIZE as may be drawn against its Number, according to a Resolution of CONGRESS, passed at Philadelphia, November 18, 1776." Signed by "Jo: Budden" with a class indicator "S." at lower left. The Continental Congress authorized this lottery—widely regarded as the first national lottery in American history—on November 18, 1776, to help finance the Revolutionary War. The lottery was organized in classes with tickets sold at $20 each, and administered by commissioners including James Searle, John Bayard, and Daniel Roberdeau of Philadelphia. Jo: Budden served as a lottery manager responsible for signing the tickets. A later collector annotation notes this ticket as "Very rare," reflecting the extreme scarcity of surviving Continental Lottery tickets.

title: Anglo-American Tontine Plan, 1789: Prospectus (Page 1 of 3)
description: This printed prospectus describes "A PLAN OF A TONTINE to consist of 1000 Shares of £.100 each, to be divided into Seven Classes, as follow; the Dividend of each Class to increase by Survivorship." Seven age classes range from lives under 10 (170 shares at £6 2s per £100) to lives above 60 (100 shares at £8 3s per £100), totaling capital of £6,679. Subscribers are to pay £50 per £100 subscribed by November 1789 to Messrs. Lockhart, Bankers, Pall-Mall, with the remaining 50% due January 1790 when each subscriber must give the name, age, and description of their nominee. To secure the annuities, the prospectus pledges $600,000 in United States government funds (equal to £135,000 sterling at 6% per annum) vested in the names of trustees Francis Baring, Edmund Boehm, and Thomas Henchman of London—plus £50,000 in Three per Cent Consolidated Annuities of Great Britain. This Anglo-American tontine, backed directly by US federal bonds, exemplifies the transatlantic financial connections between Britain and the newly independent United States formed immediately after the Revolutionary War.

title: Paine, The Decline and Fall of the English System of Finance – Cover (Page 1 of 23)
description: This image shows the front cover wrapper of Thomas Paine's pamphlet "The Decline and Fall of the English System of Finance," printed in Philadelphia in 1796. The worn, water-stained paper cover bears the title in plain typography: "THE DECLINE AND FALL OF THE ENGLISH SYSTEM OF FINANCE." Library call numbers "W0.30731" and "3408" are written at the top, indicating a Yale University holding. Paine wrote this work in France in 1796 to argue that the English system of funded national debt and paper bank notes was on a mathematically calculable path to collapse—a prediction partially vindicated the following year when the Bank of England suspended cash payments in 1797.

title: Jacob Henriques: Proposed Lottery Scheme, London, April 1753
description: This printed broadside by Jacob Henriques of London, dated April 1753, proposes a new lottery scheme of 100,000 tickets at four pounds each, to be drawn by quadruple numbers so that 25,000 numbers will determine the whole lottery. The prize scheme offers: 4 prizes of £10,000, 8 of £5,000, 40 of £1,000, 130 of £500, 340 of £100, 800 of £50, 2,000 of £20, and 12,000 of £10, totaling 15,272 prizes of £394,000 with 84,728 blanks (a ratio of only five and a half blanks per prize). An additional £6,000 is distributed to the first and last drawn tickets. Twelve per cent is to be deducted from prizes for the intended purpose and paid either in money or in annuities at 3 per cent. Henriques argues that "My Proposal for lessening the national Debt is real Good, and my Intention for it is still better; and if the Parliament and Government are pleased for the Sake of their Community to encourage me with their prudent public Approbation, I will find out other great Matters for the Glory, Honour, and Welfare of his excellent Majesty." Beinecke Library call number annotations "65.802" and "cw" appear at top right.
creator: Jacob Henriques
notes: Jacob Henriques, proposed lottery scheme broadside, London, April 1753. 100,000 tickets at £4 each.

title: Ancient Papyrus: Greek or Coptic Cursive Financial or Legal Document (Ptolemaic–Byzantine Egypt, ca. 1st–7th century CE)
description: This fragment of an ancient papyrus bears cursive script in Greek or Coptic, consistent with documents produced in Egypt during the Ptolemaic, Roman, or Byzantine period (ca. 3rd century BCE to 7th century CE). The text appears to be a financial or administrative document—possibly a tax receipt, grain account, loan agreement, or land lease—of the type commonly produced in pharaonic and Hellenistic administrative practice. Papyri of this kind constitute the oldest surviving form of financial documentation in the Western world and are among the earliest physical evidence of organized credit, taxation, and contractual obligation. This is likely the oldest item in the collection.
creator: Unknown (Egyptian administrative scribal office)
notes: Ancient papyrus fragment with cursive Greek or Coptic script. Likely a financial or administrative document (tax receipt, grain account, or land lease). Ptolemaic, Roman, or Byzantine Egypt, ca. 1st–7th century CE. Oldest item in the collection.

title: New York Central Rail Road Company: $1,000 Bond at 6% (No. 1890, August 1858) [Cancelled]
description: This engraved bearer bond of $1,000 at 6% annual interest was issued by the New York Central Rail Road Company. No. 1890. Dated August 1858. Stamped CANCELLED. The New York Central Rail Road was created in 1853 by the merger of several smaller railroads connecting Albany to Buffalo and was one of the flagship lines of the Cornelius Vanderbilt railroad empire. At 6% interest, this bond was issued during a period when American railroads were paying relatively high rates to attract capital. The CANCELLED stamp indicates this bond was redeemed or retired before the company reorganized into the New York Central and Hudson River Railroad in 1869.
creator: New York Central Rail Road Company
notes: New York Central Rail Road Company. $1,000 bond, 6%. No. 1890. August 1858. CANCELLED.

title: Spanish Colonial Government: Libramento (Payment Order) No. 444 – Lima, Peru, October 1816 [Empréstito Patriótico]
description: This handwritten document is a libramento (government payment order/draft) No. 444 issued by the Spanish colonial treasury in Lima, Peru, in October 1816. Related to the "Empréstito Patriótico" (Patriotic Loan), an emergency forced loan imposed by the colonial government on the population of Lima to finance the royalist war effort during the South American Wars of Independence. Son 500 pesos. The document specifies a remarkably high annual interest rate of 25%. An additional notation from 1817 attests to further processing or partial payment. At the time of issue, the Vice-royalty of Peru was one of the last remaining strongholds of Spanish colonial rule in South America; independence would come only in 1821. This document is a rare surviving financial instrument from the final phase of Spanish colonial governance in Peru.
creator: Spanish Colonial Treasury, Lima (Tesorería de Lima)
notes: Spanish colonial libramento No. 444. Lima, Peru, October 1816. Empréstito Patriótico (forced loan). 500 pesos at 25% annual interest. Additional notation from 1817.

title: Imperial Russian Government: Internal 5% Prize Loan (Билет Внутреннего 5% с Выигрышами Займа), 100 Rubles (Series 15871, No. 45, 1864)
description: This bilingual (Russian/German) printed bearer lottery bond of 100 rubles at 5% interest with prize draws (выигрыши) is from the Internal 5% Prize Loan of 1864. Russian title: Билетъ Внутреннего 5% с Выигрышами Займа. German title: "Obligation der Russischen 5% Inneren Anleihe mit Prämien-Verlösungen von 1864." Series 15871, No. 45. 1864. Prize loans (займы с выигрышами) combined regular interest payments with periodic lottery drawings offering large cash prizes (выигрыши), making them more attractive to small and medium investors than plain bonds. They were widely used by European governments in the nineteenth century. The bilingual German text indicates this domestic loan was also actively traded in German financial markets.
creator: Imperial Russian Government
notes: Imperial Russian Government Internal 5% Prize Loan 1864. 100 rubles. Series 15871, No. 45. Bilingual Russian/German. Lottery prize-bond format.

title: Thomas C. Jenkins, Wholesale Dealer in Flour & Groceries: Invoice to Offutt & Son (Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, December 19, 1902)
description: This printed commercial invoice is from Thomas C. Jenkins, described as "The Largest Flour and Grocery House in the World," located at Nos. 508–522 Penn Avenue and Nos. 509–523 Liberty Street, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, at the Foot of Fifth Avenue near the Pittsburgh Market. The invoice is dated December 19, 1902, and addressed to Offutt & Son. Items listed include tobacco products: "J.S. Toba[cco] 63" (36 units at $2.68) and "Island Navy [tobacco] 56" (36 units at $2.016). Note: "No Goods Sold Families. No Retail Store or any Connection with any Retail House in any Shape or Form. All Electric Line Cars run to or pass near the New Building." SHIPPED DIRECT stamp. Jenkins' establishment handled flour, groceries, teas, roast coffees, and tobaccos on a wholesale basis, and was a major commercial presence in late Gilded Age Pittsburgh.
creator: Thomas C. Jenkins (Wholesale Dealer in Flour & Groceries)
notes: Thomas C. Jenkins, Pittsburgh, PA. Wholesale Flour & Groceries. Invoice to Offutt & Son, December 19, 1902. Items: tobacco products. Nos. 508–522 Penn Ave. / 509–523 Liberty St. "The Largest Flour and Grocery House in the World."

title: Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company: Blank Check, Virginia City, Montana Territory (No. 224S, ca. 1866)
description: A blank (unused) check No. 224S from the Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company, drawn at Virginia City, Montana Territory. Payable to order in Dollars, signed by John Russell & Co., William, N.Y., acting as Treasurer and Agent of the Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company. The check features an engraved vignette of a frontier scene and a stagecoach. Ben Holladay's Overland Mail and Express Company was the dominant stagecoach and mail carrier in the American West in the 1860s, holding government mail contracts across transcontinental routes before Holladay sold the enterprise to Wells, Fargo & Co. in 1866. Unused financial paper from frontier-era western transportation finance.
creator: Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company
notes: Blank check No. 224S. Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company. Virginia City, Montana Territory. Signed by John Russell & Co., William, N.Y., Treasurer. Ben Holladay's stagecoach enterprise; sold to Wells Fargo 1866.

title: Willem, Prince of Orange-Nassau: Archaic Dutch Princely Bond / Obligation (Netherlands, 17th Century)
description: A printed archaic Dutch-language bond or financial obligation beginning "Y VVILHELM: BY DER GRATIE GODES, Prince van [Vernorde-der-dijck]..." referencing the Prince of Orange-Nassau and financial obligations associated with his treasury (Tresorier General and Rentmeester). The document references the name "FREDERICUS HENRICX, Prince van Oraenien" (Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange, 1584–1647) and appears to concern a princely debt obligation, potentially related to the fiscal administration of the Orange-Nassau domains. The archaic spelling and typography suggest a mid-17th century document. This is a rare and historically important early modern sovereign financial instrument from the Dutch Golden Age.
creator: Prince of Orange-Nassau (Willem / Frederick Henry)
notes: Archaic Dutch princely bond/obligation. "Y VVILHELM BY DER GRATIE GODES, Prince van [?]..." References FREDERICUS HENRICX (Frederick Henry), Prince of Orange. Tresorier General and Rentmeester noted. Mid-17th century Dutch Golden Age fiscal document.

title: Wy Gustavus, Koning van Sweeden: Royal Loan Bond, 400,000 Rijksdaalders Hollandsch Courant (Amsterdam, February 9, 1782)
description: A printed royal Swedish government bond beginning "Wy GUSTAVUS, door Gods Genade Koning van Sweeden, der Gothen en Wenden &c. &c. &c., Erfheer van Noorwegen, Hertog van Schleswyck, Hollstein, Stornarn en Ditmarschen, Grave van Oldenburg, Delmenhorst &c. &c." Issued in Amsterdam on February 9, 1782. The bond is for Vierhonderd Duizend Rijksdaalders Hollandsch Courant Geld (400,000 Rijksdaalers Dutch currency), raised through the Amsterdam money market via Johannes Verge [?] as agent and translator. The document sets out the terms of the Swedish royal loan, interest payments, and the security provided by the Swedish Crown. King Gustav III of Sweden (reigned 1771–1792) frequently borrowed in the Amsterdam capital market to finance his ambitious domestic and foreign policies, including the Russo-Swedish War of 1788–1790.
creator: King Gustav III of Sweden
notes: Swedish royal loan bond, King Gustav III. 400,000 Rijksdaalders Hollandsch Courant. Amsterdam, February 9, 1782. Issued through Amsterdam money market. Johannes Verge, agent/translator. Gustav III borrowed extensively in Amsterdam to finance Swedish policy.

title: Potosian Land Grant: Reverse Side / Back of 400 Acre Certificate (London, ca. 1825)
description: The reverse side (back) of a Potosian Land Grant certificate for 400 Acres. The reverse shows the ornate printed header of the certificate featuring the POTOSIAN LAND GRANT title, a central coat of arms or heraldic vignette (400 Acres / 400 Acres), and handwritten endorsements or signatures at the bottom (including initials "RW," "AS," and a further signature "W.R. Ramos" or similar). The Potosian Land Grant certificates were issued by a British company in London ca. 1825 as part of speculative investment schemes in the Bolivian Potosí region following South American independence. This reverse side complements the front face of the certificate (see goetzmann0506), together comprising the complete land grant document.
creator: Potosian Land Grant Company (London)
notes: Potosian Land Grant, reverse/back side of 400-acre certificate. Shows printed header with coat of arms and handwritten endorsements. Complements front face (goetzmann0506). London, ca. 1825. British speculative investment in Bolivian Potosí region.

title: French Princes in Exile (Louis Stanislas Xavier & Charles Philippe): Obligation No. 34, f. 1,000 Dutch Guilders (Amsterdam, September 28, 1793)
description: A remarkable historical bond document issued under authority of the French Princes in exile, bearing the authorization of LOUIS STANISLAS XAVIER (the future King Louis XVIII) and CHARLES PHILIPPE (the Comte d'Artois, future King Charles X), countersigned by Le Maréchal, Duc de Broglie (from Hamm en Westphalie, signed on the twenty-fifth day of September 1793). No. 34. The Amsterdam house of J. Bourcourd and Wedowe F. Croese & Comp. certifies receipt, on behalf of Their Royal Highnesses the Princes of France, from an unnamed party, of the sum of EEN DUIZEND GULDENS Hollandsch Courant (f. 1,000 Dutch guilders) in exchange for a share in the stated Obligation, with corresponding coupons attached. Amsterdam, September 28, 1793. Notarized and authenticated. This bond was issued by the French royal family members during their exile following the execution of Louis XVI, as a means of raising funds in the Dutch capital market for the Royalist cause.
creator: Louis Stanislas Xavier (Louis XVIII); Charles Philippe (Comte d'Artois); Maréchal Duc de Broglie
notes: French émigré princes' bond No. 34, f. 1,000 Dutch Guilders. Authorized by Louis Stanislas Xavier (future Louis XVIII) and Charles Philippe (Comte d'Artois). Duc de Broglie, Hamm en Westphalie, September 25, 1793. Amsterdam, J. Bourcourd & Wedowe F. Croese & Comp., September 28, 1793. French Royalist exile finance.

title: Ricardo & De Lara (Amsterdam): Forward Contract for British Consolidated Stock, £[?] (Amsterdam, May 13, 1805)
description: A handwritten forward sale contract (Dutch-language) arranged through Ricardo en De Lara, Warpenfort over het Wardhuis, No. 70, Amsterdam. The undersigned commits to sell a quantity of Pounds Sterling of the Consolidated Stock of Great Britain (British Consols / Geconsolideerde Schuld van [?] Ann [?]) at a set price, to be delivered at the Recontre (settlement date) in London at the agreed price. All deliveries and conditions are governed by the December 1803 Reglement referenced. Amsterdam, May 13, 1805. Signed by the counterparty. The Ricardo firm in Amsterdam (related to the famous Ricardo family including economist David Ricardo) was a leading broker in Anglo-Dutch financial transactions, facilitating the forward trading of British government bonds in Amsterdam — a major center for international consol dealing.
creator: Ricardo & De Lara (Amsterdam)
notes: Amsterdam forward contract for British Consolidated Stock (Consols). Ricardo en De Lara, No. 70 Amsterdam. Governed by December 1803 Reglement. Amsterdam, May 13, 1805. Ricardo family Amsterdam firm; Anglo-Dutch financial intermediation.

title: Conditien van Negotiatie, Daniel Changuion, Essequibo & Demerara, Article 1, f.400,000 at 6%, 1816

title: Notarial Copy - Heshuysen & Compagnie / James Balmer, Dominica Plantation Paaschal Mortgage, Amsterdam, December 21, 1777
description: Notarial copy (Copie) of a complex financial transaction recorded before notary Hermanus de Wolff Junior, Amsterdam, December 21, 1777. Concerns a mortgage (Mortgage of Hypotheeq) on Plantation Paaschal, situated on the island of Dominica, originally established August 13, 1772, by James Balmer, London merchant, for the benefit of Adolf Jan Heshuysen, Floris Visscher Heshuysen, and Frans Jacob Heshuysen, composing the firm Adolf Jan Heshuysen en Compagnie, Haarlem. The mortgage secures a sum of 7,100 pounds sterling. The plantation had earlier been encumbered with life annuities of 1,269 pounds and 10 shillings per annum on various lives in Great Britain. The document records the formal presentation of this mortgage before the Amsterdam notary.
creator: Hermanus de Wolff Junior (Notaris); Adolf Jan Heshuysen en Compagnie
notes: Amsterdam notarial copy; Plantation Paaschal, Dominica; Heshuysen & Comp. / James Balmer; mortgage £7,100; life annuities £1,269.10/yr; notary Hermanus de Wolff Junior; December 21, 1777

title: Notarial Copy - Heshuysen & Compagnie / James Balmer, Dominica Plantation Paaschal Mortgage, Amsterdam, March 21, 1777
description: Notarial copy (Copie) of an earlier stage of the same Heshuysen/Balmer Dominica plantation mortgage transaction, recorded before notary Hermanus de Wolff Junior, Amsterdam, March 21, 1777. Also concerns the mortgage on Plantation Paaschal (Dominica) by James Balmer, London, for the benefit of Adolf Jan Heshuysen, Floris Visscher Heshuysen, and Frans Jacob Heshuysen (Adolf Jan Heshuysen en Compagnie, Haarlem), with reference to the same mortgage of August 13, 1772. This document predates the December 1777 notarial copy by nine months and represents an earlier phase of the same complex transaction.
creator: Hermanus de Wolff Junior (Notaris); Adolf Jan Heshuysen en Compagnie

title: 3½% Engeland War Loan 1932, Dutch Certificaat aan Toonder SPECIMEN, £100, Amsterdam (Twintigste Trust-Maatschappij)
description: Specimen Dutch bearer certificate (Certificaat aan Toonder) No. 15145 for £100, representing holdings in the British Government 3½% England War Loan 1932. Issued by the Administratiekantoor van De Twintigste Trust-Maatschappij (The Twentieth Trust Company) in Amsterdam. Marked "SPECIMEN." The certificate was issued under administration conditions available free of charge at the issuing office. Interest (coupons) payable June 1 and December 1. Administered by Algemeen Kantoor van Administratie te Amsterdam B.V. The England War Loan 1932 was the British government's landmark conversion of WWI 5% War Loan to 3½%, the largest ever voluntary debt conversion.

title: Republica Boliviana, Empréstito Interior, Vale de Cien Pesos at 6%, Chuquisaca, 1827–1828
description: Bolivian Republic domestic debt certificate (Vale) for 100 Pesos from the Empréstito Interior (Internal Loan) of one million pesos, authorized by the Constituent Congress law of November 16, 1826. Bears 6% annual rent payable in thirds in January, May, and September. Negotiable and accepted by republican treasuries at face value for purchase of public properties and reduction of censos, per decrees of June 12, 1827. Threatens death penalty for counterfeiters ("Pena de Muerte al Falsificador y Cómplices"). Issued at Chuquisaca (now Sucre), July 24, 1827. Departmental annotation from Chuquisaca, February 7, 1828 (the 17th year of Independence). Endorsed to Ramon Molina, annotated at folio No. 22. Signed by the Ministro de Hacienda, El Prefecto, and El Administrador del Tesoro.
creator: República Boliviana, Ministerio de Hacienda

title: Bolivian Government Loan of 1872, Trust Certificate for Bond No. 242, £400, June 1880
description: Trust certificate issued by John Horatio Lloyd and Alfred James Lambert, Trustees of the net proceeds of the 5% Government Loan raised in 1872 by the Republic of Bolivia, certifying that the holder is entitled to a rateable proportion of the ultimate balance of the trust fund. The trust was established from proceeds intended for payment of public works as described in the original loan prospectus. Covers Bond No. 242, dated February 12, 1872, for £400. Payment of the ultimate proportion of the trust fund balance will be notified by advertisement when it becomes available for distribution. Dated June 1880. A note at the bottom references the judgment of Her Majesty's Court of Appeal (June 1879) in the action "Wilson v. Church and others" and the House of Lords judgment (March 1880) in the action "National Bolivian Navigation Company and others v. Wilson."
creator: Trustees: John Horatio Lloyd & Alfred James Lambert
notes: Bolivian 5% Government Loan 1872; trust certificate; Bond No. 242, £400, dated February 12, 1872; trustees Lloyd & Lambert; legal references to Wilson v. Church (Court of Appeal 1879, House of Lords 1880)

title: Imperial Brazil, Conversion and Redemption Circular for 5% Loans of 1865–1886, £20,000,000 at 4%, N.M. Rothschild & Sons, 1888
description: Printed circular document announcing the Conversion and Redemption of the Imperial Brazilian Five Per Cent Loans of 1865, 1871, 1875, and 1886. Issue of £20,000,000 in new 4 per cent Brazilian Bonds, proceeds to be applied exclusively to conversion and redemption of the above loans. Issued under authority of Emperor of Brazil under Laws No. 3,329 (September 3, 1884) and Nos. 3,396 and 3,397 (November 24, 1888). N.M. Rothschild & Sons announce readiness to receive subscriptions. Conversion terms: 4% Bonds issued at 90, exchanged for old 5% bonds at various premiums; interest begins from first half-yearly coupon after January 1, 1889. Old bonds not presented for conversion will bear 3% from October 1, 1889.
creator: N.M. Rothschild & Sons (agents for Imperial Brazil)

Presented in both English and French with Arabic title text and decorative Moorish/Islamic arch motif framing the certificate. Cairo, January 1904. Has endorsement and transfer stamps visible at bottom. The Egyptian Credit Foncier provided real estate and agricultural mortgage credit in Egypt under the Khedival era.
creator: Egyptian Credit Foncier / Crédit Foncier Egyptien
notes: Egyptian Credit Foncier; Khedival decree February 15, 1880; No. 5,000,246; £20 / Frs. 500 bearer share; capital £4,000,000; Cairo January 1904; Islamic arch motif; bilingual English/French with Arabic; transfer stamps

title: Bank of England Transfer Receipt, Consolidated £3 per Cent. Annuities, £50, Chester, 1866

The annuity was erected by an Act of Parliament of the 25th Year of the Reign of King George II. Printed form with manuscript entries. Two witness signatures. Anti-fraud notice on right.
creator: Bank of England
notes: Consolidated 3% Annuities, £50 transfer to Miss Mary Graham, Chester; consideration £43.18.9. August 7, 1866. Brokers stamp at right. Printed by Walter John Coe, Superintendent, Bank of England.

The document authorizes named attorneys to accept transfers of Three per Cent. Annuities at the Bank of England and to receive dividends on behalf of the principals. Printed by the Bank of England. London seal affixed. Signed, sealed, and delivered in the presence of witnesses. Standard printed form with manuscript insertions.
creator: Bank of England
notes: Joint Account No. 17601, Letter of Attorney for Consolidated 3% Annuities; issued to James Goldson and others, ca. 1831. Names multiple attorneys for dividend receipt and transfers at Bank of England.

title: Latin American Government Bond, Coupon Sheet and Conditions, 10-Unit Coupons (verso), 19th century
description: Reverse side (verso) of a 19th-century Latin American government bearer bond. The number of coupons and artistic style are consistent with a long-term bond issued by a Latin American government (possibly Mexican, Argentine, or Peruvian) in the mid-to-late 19th century. Recto (face) not visible in this image.

title: Chilian Eastern Central Railway Company, Limited, First Mortgage Gold Bond, $20 / Frs. 503, No. 7645, cancelled
description: Bearer first mortgage gold bond (Obligation de Premiere Hypotheque au Porteur) of The Chilian Eastern Central Railway Company, Limited (Compagnie du Chemin de Fer de l'Est Central Chilien), No. 7645. Denomination: $20 (twenty US dollars) / Frs. 503 (five hundred and three Swiss/French francs) each. Total capital: $2,100,000. Issue of 4/625 Five per Cent First Mortgage Gold Bonds of $20 each. Bilingual French and English. Cancelled by hole-punch. Signed by company officers.
keywords: Compagnie du Chemin de Fer de l'Est Central Chilien
creator: The Chilian Eastern Central Railway Company, Limited
notes: Chilian Eastern Central Railway Company, Limited (Compagnie du Chemin de Fer de l'Est-Central Chilien), First Mortgage Gold Bond, $20 / Frs. 503, No. 7645. Issue of 41,625 bonds at $20 each; capital $3,210,000. Bearer. Cancelled (holes punched). 2-page document; see goetzmann0692 for verso.

title: Chilian Eastern Central Railway Company, Limited, First Mortgage Gold Bond No. 7645 (verso and conditions of issue)
description: Reverse side (verso) of The Chilian Eastern Central Railway Company, Limited First Mortgage Gold Bond No. 7645 (see goetzmann0691 for recto). The left portion shows The Conditions of Issue in small printed text. The right portion shows the back label: The Chilian Eastern Central Railway Company, Limited, First Mortgage Bond, $20, No. 7645. Cancelled by hole-punch.
creator: The Chilian Eastern Central Railway Company, Limited
notes: Chilian Eastern Central Railway Company, Limited, First Mortgage Gold Bond No. 7645, verso and conditions of issue. $20 / Frs. 503. Cancelled. See goetzmann0691 for recto.

title: Kingdom of Serbia, 4% State Bond, 500 Dinars / 500 Francs Bearer Bond with Talon, ornate color illustration, ca. late 19th century
description: Ornately illustrated bearer bond of the Kingdom of Serbia, 4% series, denomination 500 dinars / 500 francs, with attached talon (coupon renewal stub). The bond face features elaborate full-color Art Nouveau allegorical artwork depicting female figures representing agriculture and industry, with pastoral landscape scenes. The talon portion on the left contains multilingual text (Serbian and French at minimum). The artistic quality and style are consistent with late 19th-century Serbian government bonds printed by a major European security printing firm.
creator: Kingdom of Serbia
notes: Kingdom of Serbia, 4% State Bond, 500 Dinars / 500 Francs, bearer. Ornate color illustration with allegorical figures. Talon (coupon renewal receipt) attached at left. Bilingual Serbian/French.

title: Kingdom of Serbia, 4% State Bond (verso), showing lottery/amortization draw tables and multilingual conditions
description: Reverse side (verso) of the Kingdom of Serbia 4% bearer bond (see goetzmann0693 for recto). Shows lottery/amortization redemption draw tables listing bond numbers, multilingual conditions of issue in Serbian and French, and repeated talon labels. The text "4 obligacije" (4 obligations) is visible in Serbian script. The amortization tables list bond numbers drawn for redemption in successive lottery draws.
creator: Kingdom of Serbia
notes: Kingdom of Serbia, 4% State Bond, verso. Lottery/amortization draw tables and multilingual conditions of issue. 2-page document; see goetzmann0693 for recto.

title: Amortization / Redemption Lottery Plan, Trilingual (French / German / Serbian), Kingdom of Serbia 4% Bond, ca. late 19th century
description: Trilingual amortization and redemption lottery plan (Tableau d'Amortissement / Amortisements-Plan / Tilgungs-Plan) associated with a Kingdom of Serbia 4% government bond. The document consists of five columns of lottery draw tables listing bond numbers drawn for redemption at successive draws. Two text panels at bottom contain the conditions of drawing in French and German respectively, each signed by authorized representatives. Likely the amortization schedule accompanying the Kingdom of Serbia 4% amortisable bond (see goetzmann0696).
currency: French franc; Serbian dinar; German mark
creator: Kingdom of Serbia
notes: Kingdom of Serbia, 4% Bond, Amortization/Redemption Lottery Plan. Trilingual: Tableau d'Amortissement (French) / Amortizacioni Plan (Serbian) / Tilgungs-Plan (German). Separate schedule sheet accompanying the bond.

title: Kingdom of Serbia, 4% Amortisable Bond, 500 Francs / 500 Dinara / 500 Franken Bearer Bond, No. 033,596, Trilingual
description: Bearer amortisable bond (Obligation / Обавезница / Obligation) of the Kingdom of Serbia (Royaume de Serbie / Kraljevina Srbija / Konigreich Serbien), 4% series, No. 033,596. Denomination: 500 Francs / 500 Dinara / 500 Franken, equivalent to R.M. 405 = Florin 200. Total loan represented by 119,014 obligations. Trilingual text in French, Serbian (Cyrillic), and German. Interest at 4% per annum. Ornate black printed border with Serbian royal coat of arms. Two orange fiscal stamps affixed. Terms of issue printed in all three languages.
currency: French franc; Serbian dinar; German mark
creator: Kingdom of Serbia
notes: Kingdom of Serbia, 4% Amortisable Bond, No. 033,596. 500 Francs / 500 Dinara / 500 Franken, bearer. Trilingual layout: French / Serbian / German. Total issue: 118,516 obligations = 59,258,000 francs. Cancelled with red and orange stamps.

title: Chinese Imperial Government, 4.5% Gold Loan of 1898, £100 Sterling Bearer Bond, Deutsch-Asiatische Bank, cancelled
description: Bearer bond (Bond for £100 Sterling / Obligation uber £100 Sterling) of the Chinese Imperial Government for the 4.5% Gold Loan of 1898 (Kaiserlich Chinesische 4.5% Staatsanleihe in Gold von 1898). Total nominal loan: £1,600,000 Sterling. Bilingual English and German. Issued by Deutsch-Asiatische Bank. Orange/tan decorative border. Bond cancelled by hole-punch. A red label affixed to the center. Part of a series of Chinese imperial loans issued with German bank intermediation in the late Qing era.
creator: Chinese Imperial Government / Deutsch-Asiatische Bank
notes: Chinese Imperial Government, 4½% Gold Loan of 1898, £100 Sterling bearer bond. Deutsch-Asiatische Bank. Total issue: 1,600,000 Sterling. Cancelled (holes punched). 2-page document; see goetzmann0698 for verso and conditions.

title: Chinese Imperial Government, 4.5% Gold Loan of 1898, £100 Bond No. 076230 (verso) - Extracts, Amortization Table, and Conditions
description: Reverse side (verso) of Chinese Imperial Government 4.5% Gold Loan of 1898 bond No. 076230 (see goetzmann0697 for recto). Left section: Extracts (English) and Auszug (German) from the loan agreement. Center: certificate label identifying bond as "Chinese Imperial Government 4.5% Gold Loan of 1898 / Kaiserlich Chinesische 4.5% Staatsanleihe in Gold von 1898, Bond / Obligation £100 Sterling, Littera C, No. 076230." Right section: Table of Drawings / Amortisations-Plan showing lottery redemption schedule. Bond cancelled by hole-punch.
creator: Chinese Imperial Government / Deutsch-Asiatische Bank
notes: Chinese Imperial Government, 4½% Gold Loan of 1898. Littera C, No. 076230. £100 Sterling. Verso: bilingual English/German extracts, Table of Drawings (amortization schedule), conditions. Interest payable 1st March and 1st September. 2-page document; see goetzmann0697 for recto.

title: Chinese Imperial Government, 4.5% Gold Loan of 1898, Interest Coupon Sheet (upper row), cancelled
description: Upper row of interest coupons from the Chinese Imperial Government 4.5% Gold Loan of 1898. Approximately 17-18 coupons arranged horizontally, each showing the 4.5% interest rate and pound sterling denomination. Printed in orange/tan with ornate engraved borders. All coupons cancelled by hole-punch. These coupons accompanied the £100 Sterling bearer bond of this loan.
creator: Chinese Imperial Government / Deutsch-Asiatische Bank
notes: Chinese Imperial Government, 4½% Gold Loan of 1898. Interest coupon sheet (upper portion), 14 cancelled coupons. Belongs to bond No. 076230; see goetzmann0697–0698.

title: Republic of Honduras, Government Railway Loan of 1869, Two 500-Franc Bearer Obligations, No. 74444, with Coupon Sheet
description: Double-bond certificate of the Republic of Honduras (Republica del Estado de Honduras) for the Honduras Government Railway Loan (Emprestito del Gobierno de Honduras / Obligation des Chemins de Fer de l'Etat). Comprises two 500-franc bearer obligations (Obligation de 500 Francs au Porteur) on a single sheet, No. 74444. Emission of 301,004 obligations of 500 francs. Coupon sheets attached on the right. Blue printing on white. Honduras coat of arms with text in French and Spanish.
creator: Republic of Honduras
notes: Republic of Honduras, Government Railway Loan of 1869. Two 500-franc bearer bonds, No. 74444. Emission of 20,000 obligations of 500 francs. French/Spanish bilingual. Paris, January 30, 1869. Large coupon sheet at right. 2-page document; see goetzmann0702 for verso.

title: Republic of Honduras, Government Loan of 1869, £12 Bond (verso) - Conditions and Back Labels
description: Reverse side (verso) of the Republic of Honduras Government Loan of 1869 double bond (see goetzmann0701 for recto). Upper portion shows the loan schedule/table and conditions of issue. Lower portion shows back cover labels identifying the bonds as "Emprunt du Gouvernement de Honduras de 1869" (French) and "Honduras Government Loan of 1869" (English), denomination £12 each, with interest payable at specified intervals and drawings triennially on 1st February and 1st August. Coupon sheet backs also visible.
creator: Republic of Honduras
notes: Republic of Honduras, Government Loan of 1869, £12 bond No. 74444, verso and conditions of issue. 2-page document; see goetzmann0701 for recto.

title: Republic of Bolivia, Emprestito de 1870 (Loan of 1870), 1,000 Pesos Bearer Bond with Coupon Sheet
description: Bearer bond (Bono al Portador) of the Republic of Bolivia (Republica de Bolivia) for the Emprestito de 1870 (Loan of 1870). Denomination: 1,000 pesos (Mil Pesos). Green/teal decorative engraved border with Bolivian coat of arms. Large attached coupon sheet on the right comprising approximately 50 interest coupons. Printed by a European security printer (possibly Italian). The loan was negotiated in 1870 and is consistent with Bolivian foreign borrowing of the early 1870s.
creator: Republic of Bolivia
notes: República de Bolivia, Empréstito de 1870 (Loan of 1870). 1,000 Pesos bearer bond. La Paz, September 5, 1870. Large full coupon sheet attached at right. Teal/green engraved design.

title: Debito Unificato 5% della Citta di Napoli (Unified 5% Debt of the City of Naples), Serie D, No. 20027, 1,000 Lire, 1881
description: Bearer bond (Cartella al Portatore / Titre au Porteur) of the Unified 5% Debt of the City of Naples (Debito Unificato 5% della Citta di Napoli), Serie D, No. 20027. Nominal capital: 1,000 lire (Capitale Nominale Lire Mille). Total emission: 80,053,310 lire. Issued under the Royal Law of 14 May 1881 (Serie 3) and Regulation approved by Decree of 15 October 1881 (Serie 3), per decision of the Consiglio Comunale di Napoli of 20-23 November 1880. Naples, 20 November 1881. Signed by Il Sindaco and Il Delegato Governativo. A new coupon sheet of 98 cedole covering 1 July 1931 to 31 December 1979 is attached, marked "Debito Nuovo Foglio Cedole 1931-1979."
creator: Municipalita della Citta di Napoli
notes: Debito Unificato 5% della Città di Napoli, Serie D, No. 20027. Capitale Nominale Lire 1,000. Bilingual Italian/French. Emission of L.It. 80,053,310. Naples, November 20, 1880. Stamped "Debito Nuovo Foglio" 1931–1975. New coupon sheet of 98 coupons valid July 1, 1931 to December 31, 1979 attached.

title: Council of Foreign Bondholders, Liberian Government 7% External Loan of 1871, Arrear Interest Certificate, Bond No. 0865, £100, London, 1899
description: Arrear interest certificate issued by the Council of Foreign Bondholders (Council House, 17 Moorgate Street, London, E.C.) representing interest in arrear amounting to £176:3:4 on Bond No. 0865 for £100 of the Liberian Government 7% External Loan of 1871. The arrear covers 1 February 1874 to 31 March 1899. Redeemable under the conditions of the Arrangement approved by the Law of the Liberian Congress of 23 January 1899. Dated 1 October 1899. Signed by the Secretary of the Council of Foreign Bondholders. Printed by Bradbury, Wilkinson & Co., Limited, London. Green decorative border.
creator: Council of Foreign Bondholders, London
notes: Liberian Government 7% External Loan of 1871. Certificate of arrear interest (£176:3:4) on Bond No. 0865 for £100. Period: February 1, 1874 to March 31, 1899. Redeemable per Arrangement approved by Liberian Congress, January 23, 1899. For the Council of Foreign Bondholders. London, October 1, 1899. Printed by Bradbury, Wilkinson & Co.

title: Republica Mexicana, 3% Consolidated External Debt Bond (Bono de la Deuda Consolidada Exterior Mexicana), Serie C, No. 621768, £200, 1890
description: Bearer bond (Bono al Portador) of the Republica Mexicana for the 3% Mexican Consolidated External Debt (Bono de la Deuda Consolidada Exterior Mexicana del 3%), Serie C, No. 621768. Year of issue: 1890. Denomination: £200 sterling / approximately $970 pesos. Green/black decorative border with Mexican eagle. Part of a series of bonds restructuring Mexico's 19th-century external debt. Signed by Mexican government officials.
creator: Republica Mexicana / Ministerio de Hacienda
notes: República Mexicana, Bono de la Deuda Consolidada Exterior Mexicana, Serie C, No. 621768. £200 / $970. 3% of 1899. Mexico City, 1899.

title: Republica Mexicana, 6% Gold Treasury Bond (Bono del Tesoro) 10 Years 1913, Primera Serie A, No. 054614, $Mex.195 / Fr.505 / £20
description: Bearer treasury bond (Bono del Tesoro al Portador) of the Federal Government of the United Mexican States (Gobierno Federal de los Estados Unidos Mexicanos), 6% Gold, 10 Years, of 1913. Primera Serie "A". No. 054614. Denomination: $Mex.195 = Fr.505 = £20 = RMK.409 = $EU.97 = FL.P.B.242. Total issue: 4,000 bonds of $Mex.195 each = 199,350 bonds of $Mex.155 = 210,000 bonds of $Mex.105 = 000,001 bonds of $Mex.65 (multiple denominations). Interest at 6% per annum. Payable 1st July and 1st January at various international financial centers. Signed by El Tesorero General de la Federacion and El Delegado Especial de Hacienda. Mexico City, 1 June 1913.
currency: Mexican peso; French franc; British pound sterling; German mark; US dollar; Dutch guilder
creator: Gobierno Federal de los Estados Unidos Mexicanos / Tesoreria General de la Federacion
notes: República Mexicana, Bono del Tesoro, 6% Oro 10 años de 1913. Primera Serie A, No. 054614. $Mex.195 / Fr.505 / £20 / RMk.409 / $EU.97 / Fl.P.B.242. Bearer. Total issue: 222,358 bonds. Mexico City, January 15, 1913. Signed by Tesorero General and Delegado Especial de Hacienda.

title: Council of Ottoman Public Debt, Provisional Receipt No. 6,951,240, 500 Livres Turques, 4% Unified Converted Ottoman Debt, Deutsche Bank Hamburg
description: Provisional bearer receipt (Recepisse Provisoire au Porteur) No. 6,951,240 issued by the Conseil de la Dette Publique Repartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman (Council of the Distributed Public Debt of the Former Ottoman Empire) for a nominal amount of 500 livres turques (Turkish pounds) from the 4% Emprunt Ottoman Dette Convertie Unifiee (4% Ottoman Unified Converted Debt), represented at Frs. 900 per Ltqs 22. Issued through Deutsche Bank und Disconto-Gesellschaft, Filiale Hamburg. Coupon receipts (also labeled Recepisse Provisoire au Porteur) attached at bottom. Issued in the context of the post-World War I settlement of the former Ottoman public debt.
creator: Conseil de la Dette Publique Repartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman
notes: Conseil de la Dette Publique Répartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman, Reçu Provisoire au Porteur No. 0,951,240. Emprunt Ottoman Dette Convertie Unifiée 4%. German paying agent: Deutsche Bank und Disconto-Gesellschaft Filiale Hamburg. Coupon sheet attached below with orange cancellation stamps.

title: Societe Ottomane des Chemins de Fer Damas-Hamah et Prolongements, 4% Bearer Bond, 500 Turkish Francs, No. 1045, Emprunt 1909 Homs-Tripoli
description: Bearer bond (Obligation au Porteur) of the Societe Ottomane des Chemins de Fer de Damas-Hamah et Prolongements (Ottoman Railway Company of Damascus-Hamah and Extensions), No. 1045, denomination 500 Turkish Francs (Francs Turcs), 4% interest. Emprunt 1909 Homs-Tripoli. Total capital: 15,000,000 francs = 660,000 L.T. (livres turques). Bilingual text in French and Ottoman Turkish (Arabic script). Purple/violet ornate border. Company is a Societe Anonyme Ottomane. Authorized representative: Banque de Paris et Pays-Bas. Conditions Generales et Garanties printed at bottom. Signed by company management.
creator: Societe Ottomane des Chemins de Fer de Damas-Hamah et Prolongements
notes: Société Ottomane du Chemin de Fer de Damas-Hamah et Prolongements (Damascus-Hama Railway), Emprunt 1909 – Homs-Tripoli. No. 41045. 500 Francs / 22 Livres Turques, 4½% bearer bond. Capital 15,000,000 francs. Bilingual French/Arabic. Stamp: Société Exécutrice, Syrie et Liban.

title: Council of Ottoman Public Debt, Bearer Receipt No. 100,014, Ltqs 2.20 / £2 / Frs. 50, Exchangeable for Serie A 1928 Annuity Bonds, Amsterdam, 1930
description: Bearer receipt (Recu au Porteur) No. 100,014 issued by the Conseil de la Dette Publique Repartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman, nominal amount Ltqs 2.20 / £2.0.0 / Frs fr. 50. The receipt is exchangeable (Echangeable) for a bearer title of Bons Representatifs des Annuites Arrieres de la Dette Publique Ottomane "Serie A" 1928. Issued under Article IV of the contract of 13 June 1928 between the Turkish Republic and holders of Ottoman Public Debt, ratified by law of 1 December 1928. Amsterdam, 22 April 1930. Issued by Banque de Paris et des Pays-Bas, par procuration. Pink/salmon colored document. Dutch revenue stamp (20c) for Amsterdam 1930 affixed.
keywords: Banque de Paris et des Pays-Bas
currency: Turkish lira; British pound sterling; French franc
creator: Conseil de la Dette Publique Repartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman / Banque de Paris et des Pays-Bas
notes: Conseil de la Dette Publique Répartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman, Reçu au Porteur No. 100,014. Ltqs 2.20 / £2.0.0 / Frs fr. 50.–. Exchangeable for Bons Représentatifs des Annuités Arriérées, Série A 1928. Amsterdam, April 22, 1930. Banque de Paris et des Pays-Bas. Dutch orange revenue stamp 20c. Printed by Imprimerie Chaix, Paris.

title: Council of Ottoman Public Debt, Bearer Receipt No. 125,515, Ltqs 4.40 / £4 / Frs. 100, Exchangeable for Serie A 1928 Annuity Bonds, Amsterdam, 1930
description: Bearer receipt (Recu au Porteur) No. 125,515 issued by the Conseil de la Dette Publique Repartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman, nominal amount Ltqs 4.40 / £4.- / Frs fr. 100. The receipt is exchangeable (Echangeable) for a bearer title of Bons Representatifs des Annuites Arrieres de la Dette Publique Ottomane "Serie A" 1928. Same issue and conditions as No. 100,014 (goetzmann0711) but double the denomination. Amsterdam, 22 April 1930. Banque de Paris et des Pays-Bas, par procuration. Dutch revenue stamp (40c) for Amsterdam 1930 affixed.
keywords: Banque de Paris et des Pays-Bas
currency: Turkish lira; British pound sterling; French franc
creator: Conseil de la Dette Publique Repartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman / Banque de Paris et des Pays-Bas
notes: Conseil de la Dette Publique Répartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman, Reçu au Porteur No. 125,515. Ltqs 4.40 / £4.– / Frs fr. 100.–. Same series as goetzmann0711; higher denomination. Amsterdam, April 22, 1930. Banque de Paris et des Pays-Bas. Dutch orange revenue stamp 40c.

title: Republica del Peru, Construction de la Aduana de Arica, 100 Soles Bearer Bond, No. 165, Lima, 1871
description: Bearer bond (Bono al Portador) of the Republica del Peru for the Construction of the Arica Customs House (Construccion de la Aduana de Arica), issued under the Law of 28 January 1869. No. 165. Denomination: 100 Soles (Cien Soles). Interest at 6% per annum and amortization at 5%, in accordance with the Law of 4 February 1869, for the construction of the Arica Customs House. Lima, 17 July 1871. Signed by Felipe Masias (?) and the Director del Credito. Teal/green decorative border with Peruvian coat of arms and allegorical vignette of figure in Andean dress.
creator: Republica del Peru / Direccion del Credito
notes: República del Perú, 100 Soles bearer bond, No. 165. Construcción de la Aduana de Arica, per law of January 28, 1869. 6% interest, 5% amortization. Lima, July 17, 1871. Signed by Felipe Medina and José Félix García, El Director del Crédito.

title: City of Warsaw, 4.5% Loan of 1931, No. 0038738, Fr. 284 / Zlot. 99.188 / Fr. suisses 57.666 Bearer Bond, Warsaw, 1931
description: Bearer bond (Obligation / Obligacja) of the City of Warsaw, Capital of Poland (Ville Capitale de Varsovie / Miasto Stoleczne Warszawy) for the 4.5% Loan of 1931 (Emprunt 4.5% 1931 de la Ville Capitale de Varsovie / 4.5% Pozyczki m. st. Warszawy Roku 1931). No. 0038738. Denomination at holder's choice: Fr. 284 (French francs) or Zlot. 99.188 (Polish zloty) or Fr. suisses 57.666 (Swiss francs). Authorized by Municipal Council of Warsaw on 10 June 1930, approved by Government Commissioner for conversion of Warsaw city bonds (10 June 1930). Total loan: Fr. 55,912,500. Interest at 4.5% per annum without deduction of Polish income tax. Warsaw, 1 June 1931. Bilingual French and Polish. Signed by the President of the City of Warsaw and a member of the Magistrat. Green decorative border with architectural vignettes of Warsaw.
currency: French franc; Polish zloty; Swiss franc
creator: Municipalite de la Ville Capitale de Varsovie / Magistrat m. st. Warszawy
notes: City of Warsaw, 4½% Loan of 1931. No. 0038738. Fr. 284 / Zł. 99¹⁸⁸ / Fr. suisses 57⁶⁶⁸. Bearer. Bilingual French/Polish. Total emission: Fr. 55,912,500 / Zł. 19,527,243. Warsaw, June 1, 1931. Approved by Commissaire du Gouvernement, June 10, 1930.

title: Republic of Poland, 3rd Series Dollar Premium Loan Bond (Obligacja), $5 / zł 44.57, No. 1222277, Warsaw, 1931
description: Bearer lottery bond (Obligacja Serji III Premjowej Pożyczki Dolarowej) of the Republic of Poland (Rzeczpospolita Polska). Series III of the Dollar Premium Loan. Face value $5 US = zł 44.57. No. 1222277. Issued pursuant to the Law of 1 February 1930. Interest at 4% per annum paid semi-annually on 1 February and 1 August. Warsaw, 1 February 1931.
creator: Republic of Poland
notes: Republic of Poland, Obligacja Serji III Premjowej Pożyczki Dolarowej (3rd Series Dollar Premium Loan Bond). No. 1222277. $5 = zł 44.57. Warsaw, February 1, 1931. Signed by Director of the Department of Monetary Circulation and Minister of Finance.

title: Republic of Poland, 3rd Series Dollar Premium Loan Bond (Obligacja), $5 / zł 44.57, No. 1450669, with Coupons 18–20, Warsaw, 1931
description: Bearer lottery bond (Obligacja Serji III Premjowej Pożyczki Dolarowej) of the Republic of Poland. Series III of the Dollar Premium Loan. Face value $5 US = zł 44.57. No. 1450669. With coupons 18, 19, and 20 attached. Warsaw, 1 February 1931. Same series as No. 1222277 (goetzmann0715).
creator: Republic of Poland
notes: Republic of Poland, Obligacja Serji III Premjowej Pożyczki Dolarowej. No. 1450669. $5 = zł 44.57. Warsaw, February 1, 1931. Same issue as goetzmann0715. Three coupons (nos. 16, 19, 20) attached at bottom.

title: Conversion of the External Debt of Portugal, Provisional Certificate of Deferred Stock, Three per Cent., London, ca. 1853
description: Provisional Certificate of Deferred Stock for the Conversion of the External Debt of Portugal under the Decree of 18th December 1852. No. 1463. Certifies the holder is entitled to a sum of Three per Cent. Deferred Stock bearing interest from 1 January 1863. To be exchanged for permanent bonds of £50, £100, £200, or £500 when sufficient stock has accumulated. Authorised by Decree of 23 March 1853. Issued by the Financial Agency of the Portuguese Government in London. Printed by Whiting, London.
creator: Financial Agency of the Portuguese Government in London
notes: Conversion of the External Debt of Portugal, Decree of December 18, 1852. Provisional Certificate of Deferred Stock. No. 1463. Three Per Cent. Deferred Stock, interest from January 1, 1863. Amount: Three Pence. Signed by Financial Agent of the Portuguese Government in London. Printed by Whiting, London.

title: Fundo Externo Portuguez (Portuguese External Fund), 3ª Série, Zero-Coupon Bond (Título Especial Sem Juro), No. 303:486, Réis 308,000 = Francos 166,67, Lisbon, 1862
description: Zero-interest bond (Título Especial Sem Juro) of the Fundo Externo Portuguez (Portuguese External Fund), 3rd Series, No. 303:486. Face value Réis 308,000 = £6-12-8 = Francos 166.67 = Marcos 135.34 = Florins 79.34. Total emission of 477,517 bonds, amortizable by public lottery. Issued by the Junta de Crédito Público, Lisbon, 22 November 1862. Stamp "remis juillet 1903" at top.
creator: Junta de Crédito Público
notes: Fundo Externo Portuguez, Título Especial sem Juro, 3.ª Série (Portuguese External Fund, Special Zero-Coupon Bond). No. 303486. title: Hope & Co. / Ketwich en Voombergh, Certificaat van Russische Fondsen in Bank-Assignatien (Certificate of Russian 6% Bank Assignations), 1,000 Rubles, No. 1326, Amsterdam, 1825
description: Bilingual Dutch/French certificate (Certificaat van 6 per Cents Russische Fondsen in Bank-Assignatien / Certificat d'Inscription Russe à 6 pour Cent en Assignations de Banque) issued by Hope en Comp., Ketwich en Voombergh, and Wed. W. Borski. No. 1326, 1,000 Rubles. Entitles the holder to an inscription of 1,000 Rubles at 6% in Russian Bank Assignations payable at the Administrative Bureau in Amsterdam. Amsterdam, 11 May 1825.
creator: Hope en Comp.; Ketwich en Voombergh; Wed. W. Borski
notes: Certificaat van 6 per Cents Russische Fondsen in Bank-Assignatien / Certificat d'Inscription Russe à 6 pour Cent en Assignations de Banque. No. 1326, R° 1000 rubles. Ten half-yearly coupons to July 4, 1829.

title: USSR State Loan for Restoration and Development of the National Economy (Государственный Заем Восстановления и Развития Народного Хозяйства СССР), 25 Rubles, Series 33 No. 012594, 1946
description: Bearer bond (Облигация) of the USSR State Loan for Restoration and Development of the National Economy. Face value 25 Rubles. Series 33, No. 012594, Class 98. Dated 1946. Post-war reconstruction bond.
creator: Government of the USSR
notes: USSR, State Loan for Restoration and Development of the National Economy (Государственный Заем Восстановления и Развития Народного Хозяйства СССР), 1946. No. 012594, Series 33, Category 98. 25 rubles. Soviet state emblem design.

title: Imperial Russian Government, State Internal 4½% Lottery Bond (Выигрышный Заём) 1917, 200 Rubles, Series 15800 No. 32
description: Bearer lottery bond (Государственный Внутренний 4½% Выигрышный Заём 1917 года) of the Imperial Russian Government (Provisional Government). Face value 200 Rubles. Series 15800, No. 32, Class 2. Issued pursuant to the resolution of the Provisional Government of 11/24 August 1917. Last coupon noted as 16 January 1928.

title: Republic of China Second Nationalist Government Lottery Loan – $5 Bond, Canton Currency (1926)

Authorized to finance construction of the Port of Whampoa (Whanpoa). Guaranteed by the revenue of the Nationalist Government with the Central Bank of China as Government Depository. Prize structure: first prize $50,000, second prizes $1,000, etc. Signed by the Director of the Revenue Department and the Minister of Finance (H.H. Kung). Text in English. Printed in green with decorative border.
creator: Republic of China, Nationalist Government
notes: Republic of China Second Nationalist Government Lottery Loan – $5 Bond, Canton Currency (1926)

title: Belgian Congo Company Share – Extraits des Statuts (Statutes Extract)
description: Extracts of the statutes (Extraits des Statuts) of an anonymous Belgian Congo company, printed in French. The company has its registered office in the Congo Belge with branches in Brussels, France, and the international union. Capital represented by 1,500 shares of 50 francs each. Louis Lambotin named as the company's first representative in the Congo. Page 1 of 2.
creator: Unknown Belgian Congo company
notes: Belgian/French company document, Extrait des Statuts (corporate statutes extract) in French; references to Louis Lambert, general assembly in Brussels, dividends and board of directors; p.1/2

description: Dividend coupon sheet for a Belgian Congo anonymous company share, showing 30 numbered coupons (nos. 1–30) in a decorative acanthus/vine oval motif, printed in black. Coupons are blank (no amounts filled in). Page 2 of 2.
creator: Unknown Belgian Congo company

Talon values: Rb. 125 and Rb. 625. Dated Amsterdam, 12 June 1924. Stamped "COUPON 1/14 JUNI 1917 BETAALD 8 cps. L.R. & Co." Page 1 of 2.

L.R. & Co." and "AAN DEN INHOUD VOLDAAN. AMSTERDAM, 19[--]" (Original receipt signed off; satisfied as to the contents). Page 2 of 2.
